narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.20—right around the typical level for students in this dataset—your law school application was evaluated using a predictive model that assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model estimated an 83% probability that you would fail the bar exam, which is high enough to trigger an automatic rejection of your admission to protect the school's outcomes and reputation. This prediction wasn't based on your race or gender, but on key aspects of your academic and personal profile that stood out as risk factors compared to others. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your planned part-time enrollment status, which is uncommon—only about 7% of students opt for part-time, and it strongly signaled higher risk to the model, likely due to patterns where part-time students face greater challenges balancing commitments. Next in importance was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 3rd, well below the average of around 5.5, indicating your performance that year lagged behind most peers and contributed noticeably to the model's concern about your bar readiness. Your family's middle-income bracket also played a supporting role in nudging the prediction toward failure, as it aligns with profiles where financial pressures sometimes impact focus and outcomes. While your LSAT score of 33 and Year 1 decile of 5 were closer to averages and didn't sway things much either way, they couldn't offset the top concerns. In comparison to typical students—who often enter full-time with stronger later-year grades and varied income backgrounds—your combination painted a picture of elevated risk that the model couldn't ignore. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a white male student with a decent academic foundation; it's the model highlighting patterns from past bar takers. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score above the 36.7 average, strengthening your Year 3 performance through targeted prep if reapplying elsewhere, or exploring full-time options at schools with flexible programs. You might also connect with advisors who can review your full profile and suggest bar prep strategies tailored to part-time schedules, turning these insights into your advantage for future applications. With focused improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor.
